claims.claim: We present a local, dependency-free controller that dynamically adjusts synthetic-user load against a bounded target service using only observable telemetry—p95 latency, error rate, a utilization proxy, and a host-memory guardrail—rather than a fixed user count.

evidence_refs.quote: # Synthetic User Load Negotiator: Adaptive Closed-Loop Pressure Control with SLO Guardrails

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: In a synthetic target model (capacity 16 concurrent service slots, p95 SLO 450 ms, request timeout 900 ms), the adaptive negotiator preserved 100% safe epochs across all experimental conditions, avoiding the unsafe overload regime that fixed high user counts (48 and 80 users) entered.

evidence_refs.quote: # Synthetic User Load Negotiator: Adaptive Closed-Loop Pressure Control with SLO Guardrails

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: However, the negotiator was conservative: over 10 epochs it achieved 96.5 steady-state requests per second (RPS), which is 6.9% below the best fixed safe baseline (static 32 users, 103.7 RPS).

evidence_refs.quote: # Synthetic User Load Negotiator: Adaptive Closed-Loop Pressure Control with SLO Guardrails

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: A longer 20-epoch run narrowed this gap to 100.5 RPS at 29 users while maintaining 100% safe epochs, still 3.0% below the best fixed safe baseline.

claims.claim: Synthetic target service.** A bounded server with configurable capacity (default: 16 concurrent service slots), stochastic request durations drawn from a truncated distribution, a FIFO queue for excess requests, and per-request timeout/SLO accounting.
